steps.title: Identify the reasoning process described in the passage
steps.content:
The key detail is that subjects imagined where the ball “would have gone if the ball that altered its path did not exist.” This is reasoning about an alternative reality — what would have happened under different conditions than what actually occurred. Each choice names a different cognitive process:
  • Choice A — ‘ambivalent’: means holding conflicting feelings about something — an emotional state, not a mode of causal reasoning. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘counterfactual’: means imagining what would have happened if actual events had been different — exactly the mental simulation described. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘analogical’: means reasoning by drawing comparisons between two similar situations; no comparison is being made here. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘associative’: means linking concepts through learned associations; the subjects are not recalling associations but simulating an alternative outcome. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: Only “counterfactual” captures the act of mentally simulating what would have occurred under different circumstances. That confirms B is correct.
